private_source_policy: The private Fractal-Mosaic manuscript is excluded from the bibliography and may be described only as the project design ontology.
selection_policy: Prefer original peer-reviewed papers, scholarly monographs, and authoritative conference proceedings; use official software documentation only in reproducibility appendices.

references.title: Hierarchical consciousness: the Nested Observer Windows model
references.ieee: J. Riddle, J. W. Schooler, “Hierarchical consciousness: the Nested Observer Windows model,” Neuroscience of Consciousness, vol. 2024, no. 1, pp. niae010, 2024. doi: 10.1093/nc/niae010.
claims_supported: Nested processing domains can be discussed across spatiotemporal scales.
Internal synchrony, inter-window coherence, and cross-frequency coupling motivate measurable multiscale variables.
claims_not_supported: Does not validate the private Fractal-Mosaic ontology or establish that OW entities are conscious.

references.title: Experimental and theoretical approaches to conscious processing
references.ieee: S. Dehaene, J.-P. Changeux, “Experimental and theoretical approaches to conscious processing,” Neuron, vol. 70, no. 2, pp. 200–227, 2011. doi: 10.1016/j.neuron.2011.03.018.
claims_supported: Large-scale availability and ignition are useful comparison concepts for global readout.
claims_not_supported: OWL global variables are not empirical neuronal workspace measurements.

references.title: An information integration theory of consciousness
references.ieee: G. Tononi, “An information integration theory of consciousness,” BMC Neuroscience, vol. 5, pp. 42, 2004. doi: 10.1186/1471-2202-5-42.
claims_supported: Structured integration and differentiation provide a comparison framework.
claims_not_supported: OWL integration is not IIT Phi.

references.title: From the phenomenology to the mechanisms of consciousness: Integrated Information Theory 3.0
references.ieee: M. Oizumi, L. Albantakis, G. Tononi, “From the phenomenology to the mechanisms of consciousness: Integrated Information Theory 3.0,” PLOS Computational Biology, vol. 10, no. 5, pp. e1003588, 2014. doi: 10.1371/journal.pcbi.1003588.
claims_supported: Formal consciousness theories can be translated into explicit mechanisms and causal structures.
claims_not_supported: OWL does not implement IIT 3.0.

references.title: The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory?
references.ieee: K. Friston, “The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory?,” Nature Reviews Neuroscience, vol. 11, no. 2, pp. 127–138, 2010. doi: 10.1038/nrn2787.
claims_supported: Prediction error, uncertainty, and homeostatic regulation motivate internal-state and action variables.
claims_not_supported: CADC is not variational free energy.

references.title: Active inference: A process theory
references.ieee: K. Friston, T. FitzGerald, F. Rigoli, P. Schwartenbeck, G. Pezzulo, “Active inference: A process theory,” Neural Computation, vol. 29, no. 1, pp. 1–49, 2017. doi: 10.1162/NECO_a_00912.
claims_supported: Perception and action may be modeled jointly under uncertainty.
claims_not_supported: RAQIC is not an implementation of active inference.

references.title: Active inference, curiosity and insight
references.ieee: K. Friston, T. FitzGerald, F. Rigoli, P. Schwartenbeck, J. O’Doherty, G. Pezzulo, “Active inference, curiosity and insight,” Neural Computation, vol. 29, no. 10, pp. 2633–2683, 2017. doi: 10.1162/neco_a_00999.
claims_supported: Information-seeking actions may have control-relevant value.
claims_not_supported: The Phase 4 epistemic head is a project-specific estimator.

references.title: A mechanism for cognitive dynamics: neuronal communication through neuronal coherence
references.ieee: P. Fries, “A mechanism for cognitive dynamics: neuronal communication through neuronal coherence,” Trends in Cognitive Sciences, vol. 9, no. 10, pp. 474–480, 2005. doi: 10.1016/j.tics.2005.08.011.
claims_supported: Phase relationships and coherence can motivate selective communication variables.
claims_not_supported: Simulation phase is not measured neural coherence.

references.title: The functional role of cross-frequency coupling
references.ieee: R. T. Canolty, R. T. Knight, “The functional role of cross-frequency coupling,” Trends in Cognitive Sciences, vol. 14, no. 11, pp. 506–515, 2010. doi: 10.1016/j.tics.2010.09.001.
claims_supported: Cross-frequency coupling motivates cross-scale coordination.
claims_not_supported: OWL does not reproduce biological oscillatory bands.

references.title: Cognitive architectures: Research issues and challenges
references.ieee: P. Langley, J. E. Laird, S. Rogers, “Cognitive architectures: Research issues and challenges,” Cognitive Systems Research, vol. 10, no. 2, pp. 141–160, 2009. doi: 10.1016/j.cogsys.2006.07.004.
claims_supported: Interacting memory, perception, action, and control modules are a standard cognitive-modeling concern.
claims_not_supported: OWL is not a general cognitive architecture.

references.title: Mathematical games: The fantastic combinations of John Conway’s new solitaire game “Life”
references.ieee: M. Gardner, “Mathematical games: The fantastic combinations of John Conway’s new solitaire game “Life”,” Scientific American, vol. 223, no. 4, pp. 120–123, 1970.
claims_supported: Simple local rules can generate complex emergent structures.
claims_not_supported: OWL extends rather than reproduces Conway Life.

references.title: Planning and acting in partially observable stochastic domains
references.ieee: L. P. Kaelbling, M. L. Littman, A. R. Cassandra, “Planning and acting in partially observable stochastic domains,” Artificial Intelligence, vol. 101, no. 1–2, pp. 99–134, 1998. doi: 10.1016/S0004-3702(98)00023-X.
claims_supported: Separating latent state from an agent’s observation is central to decision evaluation under partial observability.
claims_not_supported: OWL is not solved as a conventional POMDP.

references.title: p-adic Numbers: An Introduction
references.ieee: F. Q. Gouvêa, p-adic Numbers: An Introduction. Springer, 1997.
claims_supported: Defines p-adic valuation, norm, completion, and basic number-field structure.
claims_not_supported: Does not motivate the project’s cognitive interpretation.

references.title: Adelic model of harmonic oscillator
references.ieee: B. Dragovich, “Adelic model of harmonic oscillator,” Theoretical and Mathematical Physics, vol. 101, no. 3, pp. 1404–1412, 1994. doi: 10.1007/BF01035460.
claims_supported: Provides precedent for combining real and p-adic places in an adelic mathematical model.
claims_not_supported: Does not establish adelic cognition or biology.

references.title: p-Adic mathematical physics: The first 30 years
references.container: p-Adic Numbers, Ultrametric Analysis and Applications
references.ieee: B. Dragovich, A. Yu. Khrennikov, S. V. Kozyrev, I. V. Volovich, E. I. Zelenov, “p-Adic mathematical physics: The first 30 years,” p-Adic Numbers, Ultrametric Analysis and Applications, vol. 9, no. 2, pp. 87–121, 2017. doi: 10.1134/S2070046617020017.
claims_supported: Surveys p-adic and adelic constructions used in mathematical physics.
claims_not_supported: Survey precedent is not empirical support for the project ontology.

references.title: p-Adic Analysis and Mathematical Physics
references.ieee: V. S. Vladimirov, I. V. Volovich, E. I. Zelenov, p-Adic Analysis and Mathematical Physics. World Scientific, 1994.
claims_supported: Standard reference for p-adic analysis and mathematical-physics operators.
claims_not_supported: Not evidence of biological implementation.

references.title: Fractal Geometry, Complex Dimensions and Zeta Functions, 2nd ed.
references.ieee: M. L. Lapidus, M. van Frankenhuijsen, Fractal Geometry, Complex Dimensions and Zeta Functions, 2nd ed.. Springer, 2013.
claims_supported: Fractal strings and complex dimensions provide rigorous multiscale mathematical precedent.
claims_not_supported: OWL does not claim to implement the full fractal-string theory.

references.title: The geometry of p-adic fractal strings: A comparative survey
references.ieee: M. L. Lapidus, H. Lu, “The geometry of p-adic fractal strings: A comparative survey,” Contemporary Mathematics, vol. 551, pp. 163–206, 2011. doi: 10.1090/conm/551/10893.
claims_supported: Connects p-adic geometry with fractal-string structure and complex dimensions.
claims_not_supported: Used as mathematical background, not a cognitive model.

references.title: p-Adic numbers in physics
references.ieee: L. Brekke, P. G. O. Freund, “p-Adic numbers in physics,” Physics Reports, vol. 233, no. 1, pp. 1–66, 1993. doi: 10.1016/0370-1573(93)90043-D.
claims_supported: Review precedent for p-adic methods in physics.
claims_not_supported: Does not support cognitive or consciousness claims.

references.title: Quantum Computation and Quantum Information, 10th Anniversary ed.
references.ieee: M. A. Nielsen, I. L. Chuang, Quantum Computation and Quantum Information, 10th Anniversary ed.. Cambridge University Press, 2010.
claims_supported: Standard quantum state, circuit, measurement, and channel formalism.
claims_not_supported: Does not support literal quantum cognition in OWs.

references.title: An operational approach to quantum probability
references.ieee: E. B. Davies, J. T. Lewis, “An operational approach to quantum probability,” Communications in Mathematical Physics, vol. 17, no. 3, pp. 239–260, 1970. doi: 10.1007/BF01647093.
claims_supported: Quantum instruments provide the correct formalism for measurement outcomes plus state updates.
claims_not_supported: RAQIC is a finite project-specific construction.

references.title: States, Effects, and Operations: Fundamental Notions of Quantum Theory
references.ieee: K. Kraus, States, Effects, and Operations: Fundamental Notions of Quantum Theory. Springer, 1983.
claims_supported: Kraus operators and completely positive maps ground open-system update language.
claims_not_supported: Does not establish the project’s recursion or cognitive interpretation.

references.title: Quantum circuits with uniformly controlled one-qubit gates
references.ieee: V. Bergholm, J. J. Vartiainen, M. Möttönen, M. M. Salomaa, “Quantum circuits with uniformly controlled one-qubit gates,” Physical Review A, vol. 71, no. 5, pp. 052330, 2005. doi: 10.1103/PhysRevA.71.052330.
claims_supported: Uniformly controlled rotations support exact fixed-topology state-preparation design.
claims_not_supported: Circuit validation does not establish quantum advantage.

references.title: Doubly robust off-policy value evaluation for reinforcement learning
references.ieee: N. Jiang, L. Li, “Doubly robust off-policy value evaluation for reinforcement learning,” Proceedings of Machine Learning Research, vol. 48, pp. 652–661, 2016.
claims_supported: Doubly robust estimators combine outcome and propensity models and motivate cross-fitted factual augmentation.
claims_not_supported: Phase 3 paired branches remain the primary action-identification evidence.

references.title: Data-efficient off-policy policy evaluation for reinforcement learning
references.ieee: P. S. Thomas, E. Brunskill, “Data-efficient off-policy policy evaluation for reinforcement learning,” Proceedings of Machine Learning Research, vol. 48, pp. 2139–2148, 2016.
claims_supported: Provides precedent for data-efficient policy evaluation and uncertainty-aware estimates.
claims_not_supported: Not a drop-in proof for this simulator.

references.title: A distributional perspective on reinforcement learning
references.ieee: M. G. Bellemare, W. Dabney, R. Munos, “A distributional perspective on reinforcement learning,” Proceedings of Machine Learning Research, vol. 70, pp. 449–458, 2017.
claims_supported: Modeling outcome distributions supports risk-sensitive evaluation beyond a mean.
claims_not_supported: OWL outcomes are simulator-specific.

references.title: Distributional reinforcement learning with quantile regression
references.container: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence
references.ieee: W. Dabney, M. Rowland, M. G. Bellemare, R. Munos, “Distributional reinforcement learning with quantile regression,” Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, vol. 32, no. 1, 2018. doi: 10.1609/aaai.v32i1.11791.
claims_supported: Quantile regression motivates monotone distributional heads and lower-tail summaries.
claims_not_supported: Does not determine the project’s scalarization.

references.title: Adaptive mixtures of local experts
references.ieee: R. A. Jacobs, M. I. Jordan, S. J. Nowlan, G. E. Hinton, “Adaptive mixtures of local experts,” Neural Computation, vol. 3, no. 1, pp. 79–87, 1991. doi: 10.1162/neco.1991.3.1.79.
claims_supported: Shared representations with specialized experts motivate action-family residual models.
claims_not_supported: The Phase 4 expert architecture is project-specific.

references.title: Learning to rank using gradient descent
references.container: Proceedings of the 22nd International Conference on Machine Learning
references.ieee: C. Burges, T. Shaked, E. Renshaw, A. Lazier, M. Deeds, N. Hamilton, G. Hullender, “Learning to rank using gradient descent,” Proceedings of the 22nd International Conference on Machine Learning, pp. 89–96, 2005. doi: 10.1145/1102351.1102363.
claims_supported: Pairwise ranking losses provide precedent for learning relative candidate preferences.
claims_not_supported: Pairwise accuracy alone is insufficient without calibration/support.

references.title: Learning to rank: From pairwise approach to listwise approach
references.container: Proceedings of the 24th International Conference on Machine Learning
references.ieee: Z. Cao, T. Qin, T.-Y. Liu, M.-F. Tsai, H. Li, “Learning to rank: From pairwise approach to listwise approach,” Proceedings of the 24th International Conference on Machine Learning, pp. 129–136, 2007. doi: 10.1145/1273496.1273513.
claims_supported: Listwise objectives motivate masked normalization over executable alternatives.
claims_not_supported: The project’s action masks and losses are custom contracts.

references.title: A proportional hazards model for the subdistribution of a competing risk
references.container: Journal of the American Statistical Association
references.ieee: J. P. Fine, R. J. Gray, “A proportional hazards model for the subdistribution of a competing risk,” Journal of the American Statistical Association, vol. 94, no. 446, pp. 496–509, 1999. doi: 10.1080/01621459.1999.10474144.
claims_supported: Competing-risk methods motivate separating death/failure causes.
claims_not_supported: The Phase 4 discrete head is not necessarily the Fine–Gray model.

references.title: DeepHit: A deep learning approach to survival analysis with competing risks
references.container: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence
references.ieee: C. Lee, W. Zame, J. Yoon, M. van der Schaar, “DeepHit: A deep learning approach to survival analysis with competing risks,” Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, vol. 32, no. 1, 2018. doi: 10.1609/aaai.v32i1.11842.
claims_supported: Neural discrete-time competing-risk heads provide architecture precedent.
claims_not_supported: Survival semantics remain simulator-specific.

references.title: On calibration of modern neural networks
references.ieee: C. Guo, G. Pleiss, Y. Sun, K. Q. Weinberger, “On calibration of modern neural networks,” Proceedings of Machine Learning Research, vol. 70, pp. 1321–1330, 2017.
claims_supported: Temperature scaling and held-out calibration are standard reliability tools.
claims_not_supported: Calibration does not correct unsupported extrapolation.

references.title: Simple and scalable predictive uncertainty estimation using deep ensembles
references.container: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems
references.ieee: B. Lakshminarayanan, A. Pritzel, C. Blundell, “Simple and scalable predictive uncertainty estimation using deep ensembles,” Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, vol. 30, 2017.
claims_supported: Ensemble disagreement provides practical epistemic-uncertainty evidence.
claims_not_supported: Disagreement is only one support signal.

references.title: Conformal prediction: A gentle introduction
references.container: Foundations and Trends in Machine Learning
references.ieee: A. N. Angelopoulos, S. Bates, “Conformal prediction: A gentle introduction,” Foundations and Trends in Machine Learning, vol. 16, no. 4, pp. 494–591, 2023. doi: 10.1561/2200000101.
claims_supported: Split/Mondrian conformal methods motivate finite-sample coverage and abstention.
claims_not_supported: Coverage guarantees depend on assumptions and the implemented split.

references.title: Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters
references.ieee: V. Chernozhukov, D. Chetverikov, M. Demirer, E. Duflo, C. Hansen, W. Newey, J. Robins, “Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters,” The Econometrics Journal, vol. 21, no. 1, pp. C1–C68, 2018. doi: 10.1111/ectj.12097.
claims_supported: Cross-fitting reduces nuisance-model overfitting bias in orthogonal estimation.
claims_not_supported: Does not by itself identify simulator action effects.

references.title: Empowerment: A universal agent-centric measure of control
references.container: Proceedings of the 2005 IEEE Congress on Evolutionary Computation
references.ieee: A. S. Klyubin, D. Polani, C. L. Nehaniv, “Empowerment: A universal agent-centric measure of control,” Proceedings of the 2005 IEEE Congress on Evolutionary Computation, vol. 1, pp. 128–135, 2005. doi: 10.1109/CEC.2005.1554676.
claims_supported: Agent-centric information/control measures motivate control-relevant information value.
claims_not_supported: The Phase 4 epistemic score is not empowerment itself.

references.title: Using common random numbers for indifference-zone selection and multiple comparisons in simulation
references.ieee: B. L. Nelson, F. J. Matejcik, “Using common random numbers for indifference-zone selection and multiple comparisons in simulation,” Management Science, vol. 41, no. 12, pp. 1935–1945, 1995. doi: 10.1287/mnsc.41.12.1935.
claims_supported: Common random numbers reduce variance in paired simulation comparisons.
claims_not_supported: CRN does not eliminate model bias or interaction effects.

references.title: A comparison of three methods for selecting values of input variables in the analysis of output from a computer code
references.ieee: M. D. McKay, R. J. Beckman, W. J. Conover, “A comparison of three methods for selecting values of input variables in the analysis of output from a computer code,” Technometrics, vol. 21, no. 2, pp. 239–245, 1979. doi: 10.1080/00401706.1979.10489755.
claims_supported: Latin hypercube and stratified sampling motivate broad, controlled trait/parameter coverage.
claims_not_supported: The final population design must match the actual manifest.

references.title: Pseudoreplication and the design of ecological field experiments
references.ieee: S. H. Hurlbert, “Pseudoreplication and the design of ecological field experiments,” Ecological Monographs, vol. 54, no. 2, pp. 187–211, 1984. doi: 10.2307/1942661.
claims_supported: Repeated observations within one experimental unit do not create independent replicates.
claims_not_supported: The simulator requires its own hierarchical analysis, not a literal field experiment.

references.title: Controlling the false discovery rate: A practical and powerful approach to multiple testing
references.container: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B
references.ieee: Y. Benjamini, Y. Hochberg, “Controlling the false discovery rate: A practical and powerful approach to multiple testing,” Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B, vol. 57, no. 1, pp. 289–300, 1995. doi: 10.1111/j.2517-6161.1995.tb02031.x.
claims_supported: FDR control supports multiplicity management for secondary analyses.
claims_not_supported: Primary/secondary families must be predeclared.

references.title: Optimization of conditional value-at-risk
references.ieee: R. T. Rockafellar, S. Uryasev, “Optimization of conditional value-at-risk,” Journal of Risk, vol. 2, no. 3, pp. 21–41, 2000. doi: 10.21314/JOR.2000.038.
claims_supported: CVaR provides a coherent lower-tail risk summary.
claims_not_supported: The selected alpha and quantile approximation are project choices.

references.title: Causal Inference for Statistics, Social, and Biomedical Sciences
references.ieee: G. W. Imbens, D. B. Rubin, Causal Inference for Statistics, Social, and Biomedical Sciences. Cambridge University Press, 2015.
claims_supported: Potential-outcome language helps distinguish factual and counterfactual comparisons.
claims_not_supported: The simulator’s branch intervention has model-specific assumptions.

references.title: Homeostatic reinforcement learning for integrating reward collection and physiological stability
references.ieee: M. Keramati and B. Gutkin, “Homeostatic reinforcement learning for integrating reward collection and physiological stability,” eLife, vol. 3, art. e04811, 2014, doi: 10.7554/eLife.04811.
claims_supported: Adaptive action value can be formulated in relation to physiological or homeostatic stability rather than immediate reward alone.
claims_not_supported: The OWL homeostatic drive, setpoints, horizons, and scalarization remain project-specific.

references.title: Regression quantiles
references.ieee: R. Koenker and G. Bassett, Jr., “Regression quantiles,” Econometrica, vol. 46, no. 1, pp. 33–50, 1978, doi: 10.2307/1913643.
claims_supported: Conditional quantile modeling supports distributional outcome and lower-tail analyses beyond a conditional mean.
claims_not_supported: Quantile monotonicity and CVaR construction require explicit implementation and validation.

references.title: Selective classification for deep neural networks
references.container: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 30
references.ieee: Y. Geifman and R. El-Yaniv, “Selective classification for deep neural networks,” in Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 30, 2017, pp. 4878–4887.
claims_supported: A predictive system may trade coverage for lower supported risk by rejecting or abstaining on uncertain cases.
claims_not_supported: The Phase 4 support rule must be validated for regression/ranking and cannot inherit classification guarantees automatically.

references.title: Robust inference with multiway clustering
references.ieee: A. C. Cameron, J. B. Gelbach, and D. L. Miller, “Robust inference with multiway clustering,” Journal of Business & Economic Statistics, vol. 29, no. 2, pp. 238–249, 2011, doi: 10.1198/jbes.2010.07136.
claims_supported: Inference can account for correlated observations within more than one clustering dimension.
claims_not_supported: The final cluster dimensions and small-cluster corrections must follow the actual seed/world design.

references.title: Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models
references.ieee: A. Gelman and J. Hill, Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models. Cambridge, U.K.: Cambridge University Press, 2007, doi: 10.1017/CBO9780511790942.
claims_supported: Hierarchical models are appropriate when observations are nested within agents, worlds, seeds, or time blocks.
claims_not_supported: The final random-effects structure must be justified by the realized experiment and diagnostics.

references.title: A unified Bellman optimality principle combining reward maximization and empowerment
references.container: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32
references.ieee: F. Leibfried, S. Pascual-Díaz, and J. Grau-Moya, “A unified Bellman optimality principle combining reward maximization and empowerment,” in Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32, 2019.
claims_supported: Extrinsic outcome value and information-theoretic control can be treated as distinct but combinable objectives.
claims_not_supported: The Phase 4 epistemic head is not the same as empowerment and should not be named as such.

references.title: A mathematical theory of communication
references.ieee: C. E. Shannon, “A mathematical theory of communication,” Bell System Technical Journal, vol. 27, no. 3, pp. 379–423, and no. 4, pp. 623–656, 1948.
claims_supported: Entropy and mutual information provide formal tools for uncertainty and information gain.
claims_not_supported: Information-theoretic quantities alone do not establish cognitive competence or consciousness.
